response:
The theory of relativity, developed by Albert Einstein, is a fundamental concept in modern physics that describes the laws of motion and gravity. It consists of two parts: Special Relativity and General Relativity.

1. Special Relativity (1905):
- Special relativity deals with physics in a vacuum (no gravity present). It introduced two postulates:
  a) The laws of physics are the same for all observers moving at constant velocities relative to each other. This is the principle of relativity.
 b) The speed of light is the same for all observers, regardless of their motion. This is the principle of the constancy of the speed of light.

Example: If you and a friend are on a spaceship moving away from Earth at a constant speed. You measure the speed of light to be the same as it is measured on Earth.
